coredata.dc:title: Design of new anodic bimetallic nanocatalyst composed of Ni–Cu supported by reduced carbon quantum dots for the methanol oxidation reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Javan, Hakimeh
Asghari, Elnaz
Ashassi-Sorkhabi, Habib
coredata.dc:description: The direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC), as the ideal fuel cell system which produces the electric power by the direct conversion of the methanol fuel at the fuel cell anode. The main advantage of this kind of fuel cell is the ease of fuel transportation, an energy-dense yet reasonably stable liquid at all environmental conditions. The DMFC relies upon the oxidation of methanol on a catalyst layer to form carbon dioxide. Up to now, different particles ad nanoparticles have been used as an anodic catalysts in various researches. In this work, bimetallic nanoparticles of NiCu dispersed on reduced carbon quantum dots (RCQDs) were prepared using the facile electrochemical procedure. The electrochemical oxidation of methanol on a Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE electrode was studied in 0.50 M KOH using cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), and impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The beginning potential for electro-oxidation of methanol was observed at lower voltages for Ni-Cu/RCQD/GCE related to Ni-Cu/GCE and Ni-NPs/GCE. Also, the peak current density for the oxidation of methanol for Ni-Cu/RCQD/GCE showed a high amount of 90.41 mA.cm-2, which is threefold of Ni-Cu/GCE catalyst. EIS studies were applied to study the charge transfer rate. The charge transfer resistance for the electrochemical oxidation of methanol for Ni-Cu/RCQD/GCE was obtained 25 Ω while this amount for Ni-Cu/GCE was ca. 313 Ω.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetallic nanoparticles
Reduced carbon quantum dots
Electrochemical procedure
Electrocatalytic oxidation
Charge transfer
Charge transfer resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of electrodes by direct electrochemical reduction
Effect of scan rate on methanol oxidation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Office of the University of Tabriz

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was carried out in “Electrochemistry Research Laboratory” of “University of Tabriz”. Authors are grateful for financial supports from Research Office of the University of Tabriz .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM image of a) Ni-Cu/ GCE and b) Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE.
CV curve of three synthesized electrodes in 0.5 M KOH + 0.5 M CH3OH at a scan rate of 50 mV·s−1 at room temperature.
Tafel plots of NiCu and Ni-Cu/RCQDs in 0.5 M KOH + 0.5 M CH3OH at a scan rate of 10 mV·s−1 at room temperature.
CV curve of three synthesized Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE in 0.5 M KOH + 0.5 M CH3OH at different scan rates at room temperature.
derived plots from CV at different scan rates a) Ipeak of methanol oxidation versus ν 1/2 and b) Epeak methanol oxidation versus ν. Error bars were estimated as triple of the standard deviation.
Chronoamperomettry curve of two Ni-Cu/GCE and Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE at a constant voltage of 0.45 V vs. Ag/AgCl at 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M CH3OH.
Nyquist diagram of Ni-Cu/GCE and Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE at three different voltages at a frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M CH3OH.
Bode plot of Ni-Cu/GCE and Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE at the voltage of 0.45 V in 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M CH3OH.
Kinetic parameters of electrodes for the oxidation of methanol.
Comparison of some of the parameters for oxidation of methanol with other
extracted parameters from the fitted equivalent circuit in the Nyquist plots of Fig. 6.

head.ce:title: Design of new anodic bimetallic nanocatalyst composed of Ni–Cu supported by reduced carbon quantum dots for the methanol oxidation reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemistry Research Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electrochemistry Research Laboratory, Physical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC), as the ideal fuel cell system which produces the electric power by the direct conversion of the methanol fuel at the fuel cell anode. The main advantage of this kind of fuel cell is the ease of fuel transportation, an energy-dense yet reasonably stable liquid at all environmental conditions. The DMFC relies upon the oxidation of methanol on a catalyst layer to form carbon dioxide. Up to now, different particles ad nanoparticles have been used as an anodic catalysts in various researches. In this work, bimetallic nanoparticles of NiCu dispersed on reduced carbon quantum dots (RCQDs) were prepared using the facile electrochemical procedure. The electrochemical oxidation of methanol on a Ni–Cu/RCQDs/GCE electrode was studied in 0.50 M KOH using cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), and impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The beginning potential for electro-oxidation of methanol was observed at lower voltages for Ni-Cu/RCQD/GCE related to Ni-Cu/GCE and Ni-NPs/GCE. Also, the peak current density for the oxidation of methanol for Ni-Cu/RCQD/GCE showed a high amount of 90.41 mA.cm-2, which is threefold of Ni-Cu/GCE catalyst. EIS studies were applied to study the charge transfer rate. The charge transfer resistance for the electrochemical oxidation of methanol for Ni-Cu/RCQD/GCE was obtained 25 Ω while this amount for Ni-Cu/GCE was ca. 313 Ω.

ce:section.ce:para: The direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) has received great attention for many applications recently. As liquid fuel, methanol has some benefits like facile utilization, easy storage, and transportation [1,2]. Applications of methanol fuel cells based on nanostructures to obtain more effective clean energy is one of the energy researchers main goals, which different attempts have been done for this achievement up to now. The slow kinetics of the oxidation of methanol is one of the challenges facing scientists. Up to now, considerable attempts have been made to solve this problem. The use of alkaline solutions as an electrolyte is one of the proceedings taken to solve the mentioned challenges. Application of alkaline electrolytes has many advantages for acidic or neutral media, such as its high efficiency, the stability of different kinds of electrode materials in it, a considerable performance of both anodic and cathodic reactions, and negligible poisoning of electrode surface [3–5].

Like almost other electrochemical systems, in the methanol electro-oxidation, the material constitution of catalyst plays an essential role in the system efficiency. Different kinds of electrode materials consist of Pt, and Pt-binary compositions have been utilized as catalysts for the methanol electro-oxidation [6–8]. For the suitable performance of one electrode material, it should provide a high active surface area. For this mean, carbonic materials are commonly used as support catalysts, such as carbon, graphene, carbon nanotube, and carbon quantum dots with incorporated Pt and Pt-binary electrodes; for example, Pt- graphene [9–11], Pt- Carbon [12], Pt- SWNTs [13], Pt-CQDs [14,15], Pt-GQDs [16], PtRu-Carbon [17], Pt-Ni-graphene [18,19], Pt-Cu-graphene [20], PtCu CNT [21] etc. In recent years, many types of research have been done to reduce the use of expensive metals such as platinum, gold, palladium, etc. Although the replaced metals cannot do as well as the noble metals, their alloys with each other incorporated with carbonic support materials can be suitable candidates with good catalytic performance and efficiency [22,23].

Combining transition metals of group 8 with the group 1B results in an alloy that illustrates considerable electronic and physical properties. The improved activity of copper by nickel is not clearly understood yet. The observed activity by the NiCu alloy in catalytic systems gets interested and vital. Due to this subject, pure nickel and copper metals have the same lattice structure with similar parameters (a = 3.523 for Ni and 3.616 for Cu) caused to have different compositions of NiCu alloys. Several research types have been devoted to the chemical, physical, and electrical features of NiCu alloys [24–28]. For example, Shen et al. [29] used Nickel-Copper alloy encapsulated in graphite layers via an ambient-pressure chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method. They investigated its performance as an electrocatalyst to reduce the hydrogen in alkaline, acidic, and neutral media. Electrochemical results show that the density of the shell has an important effect on the activity of the NiCu@C. Single-layered graphene with NiCu nanoparticles presents good HER activity and stability. Another example of applying NiCu alloy as a catalyst was Yang et al. [23] synthesized the hollow CuNi alloy on RGO and studied its properties. The as-synthesized RGO–CuNi nanocomposite illustrates remarkable activity toward the glucose oxidation in alkaline solution and shows considerable activity and durability for the 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol reduction. The results from these works promoted us to use high-dispersed binary NiCu alloy on RCQDs for application in the electrochemical system of MOR for the first time.

In this work, the alloy of NiCu with incorporated of RCQDs was synthesized by facile and controllable electrochemical method in aqueous solutions. Then, the electrochemical properties of the prepared composite were studied toward electro-oxidation of methanol in alkaline media using different characterization and electrochemical techniques.

Merck Co. prepared all chemical materials consisting of Nickel sulfate (NiSO4. ·6H2O), Copper sulfate (CuSO4. ·5H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), acid boric (H3BO3), and potassium hydroxide (KOH). The electrochemical system composed of the three-electrode platinum plate, glassy carbon electrode (GCE), and an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl), respectively, as a counter, working, and reference electrodes were used for the preparation of electrodes. Electrochemical tests and the two-electrode system were used for the synthesis of CQDs.

The synthesis of CQDs was done by easy, cost-effect, and environmental-friendly electrochemical route in a two-electrode cell by the ADAK DC power described in our prior research [30,31]. However, we explain it here briefly. Two graphite rods were operated as cathode and anode and immersed in ultrapure water under applied voltages between 30‐ and 40 V. Synthesis process was continued several days to have a gray solution. The solution was then filtered by filter paper and then centrifuged (Hermle Z 36HK Tabletop high-speed centrifuge) at 22,000 rpm [32,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrodes by direct electrochemical reduction
ce:section.ce:para: Cleaning the surface of GCE is the first stage of all processes. For this mean, GCE was polished with polishing papers. Then it was degreased and rinsed with water several times. For deposition of CQDs on GCE, CQDs were dispersed in KCl (0.10 M) and CQDs (1.0 mg·mL−1) in an ultrasonic bath (BANDELIN SONOREX DIGITEC) for 40 min at pH of 4. Cyclic voltammetry was taken in the voltage between −1.5 to 0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at scan rate of 25 mV/s for 30 cycles. After that, for deposition of nanoparticles, the constant current of −0.4 mA was applied for 300 s. Sulfate salts of nickel (0.4 M NiSO4·6H2O) and copper (0.04 M CuSO4·5H2O) were used. The potassium chloride (0.1 M) and acid boric (0.3 M H3BO3) were added to the deposition bath. The mixture was dispersed in ultrasonic for 20 min to result in a homogenous solution for the later applications.

The structures and morphologies of the prepared electrodes were investigated using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (TESCAN, MIRAIII, Czech Republic). The electrochemical tests were done using an AUTO LAB PGSTAT30 potentiostat–galvanostat with a frequency response analyzer (FRA) module. Voltammetric measurements, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and chronoamperometry tests were utilized to study the electrochemical treatment of the catalysts. For the EIS analysis, the perturbation voltage was 10.0 mV (rms) in the frequency between 100 kHz to 10 mHz at constant voltages. For fitting the Nyquist plots to obtain the suitable equivalent circuits, the ZView® (II) software was used, and then corresponding parameters were extracted.

In Fig. 1a and b, SEM images of Ni-Cu/GCE and Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE are presented. The disk-shaped morphology related to nickel particles and polygons like masses of copper nanoparticles is shown in images. It is clear from these figures that underneath the CQDs layer results in better distribution and smaller size of particles [34].

To study the electrocatalytic behavior of electrodes toward oxidation of methanol in alkaline media, a cyclic voltammetry test was done, and some parameters were compared to each other. As it is evident from Fig. 2 , there are two anodic peaks during the forward and backward scan related to the oxidation and reduction of metal and a metal oxide, respectively. In addition, two prominent anodic peaks correspond to the oxidation of methanol that occurs in two steps. In fact, after the oxidation of methanol, because some produced intermediate species like CO are done imperfectly during the forward scan, this reaction is completed during a backward scan. Since the produced intermediate species cannot separate from the surface, they occupy the active sites and poison the catalyst. Because of that, the oxidation of methanol at one-step in the forward scan gets important and challenging. The peak current density ratio belongs to the forward scan, to the peak current density of the backward scan, If/Ib, is a suitable parameter for judgment about performing of reaction. The high ratio of If/Ib demonstrates better removal of the poisoning species from the surface of the catalyst. The CV analysis results show that the If/Ib amounts of the prepared NiCu nanoparticles on RCQDs is 1.29, which is larger than NiCu and Ni nanoparticles on GCE. This ratio confirms the considerable tolerance of the proposed electrocatalyst to carbonaceous intermediates [36,37]. Begging potential for oxidation of methanol is another critical determining quantity that is known as onset potential. Fig. 2 shows this quantity for Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE that is lower than that of two other electrodes. Also, the high catalytic activity of Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE electrocatalyst is informed by its high current density at peak potential. For more kinetic studies of prepared electrodes, the Tafel plots are shown in Fig. 3 . As is obvious, the calculated Tafel slope for Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE is lower than the Ni-Cu/ GCE. It can be due to the lower diffusion polarization which created by high active surface area of CQDs [34,38] (Tables 1, 2
).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate on methanol oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of scan rates (10, 30, 50, 70, 100, 150, 200 mV·s−1) was studied on methanol oxidation in 0.5 M KOH with 0.5 M methanol. As it is obvious, the peak potentials shifted to positive amounts at high scan rates that indicate the kinetic limitation in the reaction. The relation between anodic peak currents with the square root of scan rates and anodic peak potential with scan rate in a range of 10–200 mV·s−1 was derived from Fig. 4 and illustrated in Fig. 5 . As it is obvious from the CV profile, the cathodic peak is not observed at low scan rates while it gets observable at high scan rates. It means that the nickel species oxidation to a higher valence state at high scan rates is much faster than the low scan rates.

Fig. 5a illustrates the linear relation between anodic peak currents and the square root of scan rates, which means that the oxidation of methanol by catalysis of Ni-Cu/RCQDs is under diffusion control of methanol from the bulk solution to the electrode surface [44].

Chronoamperometric measurement is a suitable and common method for studying the stability of catalysts. The tests were recorded in 0.5 mol·L−1 KOH and 0.5 M CH3OH for 3600 s in 0.45 V vs. Ag/AgCl. As shown in Fig. 6 , the current densities of Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE catalyst dropped relatively slower at the initial seconds and after that reach a constant amount. A higher steady-state current of Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE catalyst is a piece of evidence for better electrocatalytic activity. This high current density can be related to the presence of CQDs. In fact, as supporting materials, CQDs caused the removal of the poisoning species such as CO from the surface of the electrode.

The Nyquist plots consist of two overlapped and depressed semi-circles in the range of applied frequency. By increasing applied voltage, the first semi-circle diameter at high-frequency range does not get affected considerably. Still, it caused a decrement in the diameter of the second semi-circle at the low-frequency range. Fig. 7 presents an equivalent circuit, where R1, CPE1, and R2 are the intrinsic resistance of solution, double-layer capacitance, and charge transfer resistance created by the electrocatalytic oxidation of methanol, respectively. During reaction occurrence at low frequency regain, some intermediate species such as CO were adsorbed on the electrode surface and occupy the active sites of catalyst and do not let more adsorption of methanol on the electrode surface. This observed process is simulated using a parallel set of a resistor: R3 and a capacitor (CPE2) in series with R2. The fitted results are presented in Table 3 .

The Bode plot of NiCu and Ni-Cu/RCQDs electrodes are also presented in Fig. 8 . As it is obvious from these curves, the semi-circles in the NiCu electrode were overlapped partially, while in Ni-Cu/RCQDs, they are extremely overlapped. This observation can be related to the high created porosity by CQDs as a support material. This treatment is confirmed by fitted CPE amounts that are recorded in Table 3.

The NiCu nanoparticles were deposited by a one-step electrochemical route at constant current on modified GCE by CQDs. The synthesized nanostructure was studied by some characterization analysis. For example, SEM image of Ni-Cu/RCQDs electrode showed the disk-shaped morphology related to nickel particles and polygons like masses of copper nanoparticles. This nanoparticles showed high distribution on RCQDs. This new and cost effective nanocatalyst that was synthesized by a minimal amount of the precious metals and materials, was used as an anode in DMFCs. CQDs leads to high dispersion of metal nanoparticles on it by providing high active surface area. This high dispersion caused high current density toward the oxidation of methanol. The enhancement of current density at forward scan and even backward scan is also observable compared to single nickel nanoparticles and binary NiCu nanoparticles that can be attributed to the synergistic effect between them. The currents of the anodic peak for the oxidation of methanol in CV curves at Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE have a linear relation with the square root of a scan rate. Choronoamperograms for the Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE in the presence of methanol indicates the irreversible process and the diffusion-controlled process. In addition, the EIS results show low resistance of Ni-Cu/RCQDs/GCE at different voltages for the oxidation of methanol. Then this prepared electrocatalyst can be a suitable and promising candidate as electrode material for MOR systems.

All authors have seen and approved the final version of the manuscript being submitted. They warrant that the article is the authors' original work, hasn't received prior publication and isn't under consideration for publication elsewhere.
1.
Hakimeh javan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation, Visualization, Investigation, Software, Validation.
2.
Elnaz asghari: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing
3.
Habib ashassi-sorkhabi: Visualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was carried out in “Electrochemistry Research Laboratory” of “University of Tabriz”. Authors are grateful for financial supports from Research Office of the University of Tabriz.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Carrette, K. Friedrich, U. Stimming, Fuel cells–fundamentals and applications, Fuel cells, 1 (2001) 5-39.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient direct-methanol fuel cell based on graphene quantum dots/multi-walled carbon nanotubes composite
